sentences.text: My name is Ruth Dinah Labovitch, born May 5, 1933, in a small village-- small town-- Korzec, in Poland.
Stern.
And I'm sorry-- I actually I was born in Czernica, near Korzec.
Czernica was the village, and Korzec was the next town to it.
In Poland-- eastern Poland.
My parents. and we were seven children-- three brothers and four sisters.
And my father had a grocery store.
And my mother Was?
Quite busy with seven children, but she also had a fabric center.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and we were a very happy family.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was second youngest.
And my sister Susan was the youngest.
So us being the second two youngest children-- so my older brothers and sisters always played with us and attended us, and always willing to help us.
And-- and I loved them all very much, and we loved each other very much.
My brothers and sisters?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'll start from the oldest.
Gerhson, Yankel, Pinie. and then sisters Bayla, Leah, myself, and Susan.
Itzchak and Genia.
Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was a great man.
He learned Torah a lot, studied the Torah, and he was teaching me how to read in Yiddish, how to read and write a little.
And he was always concerned about us and helping us.
And he was a great scholar.
All the neighbors loved him, and all the people that came to the store, they were all-- admired him and had a lot of respect for him.
My mother was a great lady.
She-- she took great care of us.
And again, all the neighbors loved her as well.
And a lot of them were Ukrainian neighbors.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she was just great.
Ah, yes, there were a few others.

[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which they would come and visit us all the time-- the Jewish families, and-- and next to our village was another village, which my auntie lived there-- my mother's sister-- her husband, and their daughter.
And so they would visit us all the time.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was a very nice little town-- very pleasant to live there.
We had a nice home-- comfortable, not very big, but you know, we all-- all the girls slept in one room, boys in another room, and-- but we had each other, and that was great.
Well, I remember on Shabbat we'd go to a synagogue to Korzec, which was the town next to Czernica.
And there we'd meet more relatives, and more people, and more Jewish people.
And they would pray and see each other, visit with each other.
In Korzec?
Yes.
There was-- there was a lot-- A?
Big Jewish population there.
And it was a nice-- nice town, nice-- and most of them were Orthodox Jews.
All the Jews I remember-- the Jewish people were Orthodox.
And my family-- definitely Orthodox.
Well, Friday night, my mother would light candles.
And-- and father would say his prayers.
And then we'd have dinner-- and a regular Shabbat dinner.
And my mother was a good cook.
I remember liking chicken a lot.
And it was happy Shabbat.
Nobody, of course, was working or going to school.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And it was a relaxing day for everybody.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and that's how we would spend Shabbat, what I can remember.
Like I say, Jewish-- Yiddish-- my father taught me.
I didn't go to school until I was seven, and the war was-- it was-- the war was broken out at that time.
And I remember just going to school very briefly, Maybe?
Just for a few months in grade one.
One of my brothers was studying at the yeshiva before the war, in Warsaw.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But when the war broke out-- before the war broke out-- he came home.
And my brothers and sisters, I guess, still were going to regular public school.
In this area, before the war was very good relationship, and-- even though it was Poland, but lot of Ukrainians lived there, and Polish people.

And, like I say, I was only, like, six years old, but I remember they were all very friendly before the war, always helpful, and always-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were always nice, so far as I can remember them.
Probably mostly with my brothers and sisters and just kids that would come to the store.
Well, I remember faintly that there was some talk.
Naturally, we never had-- I don't think we even had a radio or newspapers.
Communications was very limited.
And I think there was talk that there's going to be a war.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But nobody knew-- nobody predicted, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] you know, one like that.
And I had an Auntie who lived in-- I remember that time that she lived in America-- Auntie Shaindl.
And just before the war, I remember, she came to visit us shortly before, because I remember her.
She was with us.
And it was Auntie-- Mimi Shaindl from America.
Whether she wanted my family to come to the States-- whatever-- I don't know.
But I remember there was a lot of talk between my parents and her.
Must have been in '38.
Before the war broke out?
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Not so much in this town, not so much in our village, but I remember there was talk [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that something bad's going to happen.
Probably in the store, in the house.
I know-- I know there were some relatives in Canada.
And, again, I didn't know where.
And-- but I remember my father saying, well, he's doing well in this town and makes a good living and has a nice family.
And they just-- they didn't want to move.
It was too hard.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so they remained.
Well, being that young, I really [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] couldn't see them worried or being frightened or-- no.
Just life was going on, and every day, work.
And came Shabbat, everybody rested and enjoying the day.
So I can't really-- I'm sure they were worried.
If I'd be older, I'd probably would know more.
But being a child, being that young, you know, I didn't see nothing, nothing wrong, like nothing terrible is going to be happen.
Well, in 1939, when the war broke out, Poland was divided into half.
Eastern Poland, Germany took over.
And we were-- I'm sorry, Western Poland went to Germany.
And we were Eastern Poland.
Russia came.
Russia came in 1939.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and that's when things started to change.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Although under Russia we were still-- we were still free.
We didn't have to hide or-- or be afraid to be Jewish.
Excuse me.
I'm sorry?
Of course, when they came private businesses sort of had to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] couldn't be private anymore.
They were taking over-- like, the farms had to be like [RUSSIAN].
It's a Russian-- like, something like kibbutz.
It had to belong to the government.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and then there was talk that maybe-- I remember my parents were saying maybe we should run away back to Russia, more east to Russia.
Because this-- you know, like, they knew that Germany already took western Poland over.
So there was quite a bit of talk but, again, very little communications from newspapers-- no newspapers, no radios, no television-- of course not.
My father was still in his store, yes.
And my mother-- my mother still had that fabric center.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I myself, you know, didn't seem-- I knew that Russia was here.
And-- and then when I started school in '41-- so it was about communism already.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I still didn't seem-- like myself, being a child, I was fine.
I was fine.
I had my parents, my family.
I was fine.
I'm sorry?
Yes, yes, they were all at home.
They were all at home.
They were probably going to school, yes.
After--
After the Russians came?
It was from '39 til about June, '41-- for two years, almost, not quite.
And so life then was still, you know, quite livable.
Probably was shortage of food, shortage of clothing.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] But as far as I know.
I still had enough to eat and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
With Russia?
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yeah, like I say, they-- like, private businesses have to be taken away, and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Personal.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, as a child, I just know that it was normal.
Well, in June-- I think it was June, '41-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] no, excuse me.
Can I go just back a little bit?
Like, during the Russian [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] occupation-- whatever you'd say-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was one day, I remember, that a lot of black birds were flying over our village.
And they were, like, scary, very [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] big birds.
And I remember my mother and neighbors were saying something, something terrible is going to happen.
It's a bad sign, you know, because of the black birds.
And I-- I'm just listening.
I just didn't think nothing at that time.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

And I remember one day I was sitting outside on the porch by the house, sort of all curled up, and these huge German soldiers were marching-- I guess towards the front lines-- and our house was sort of on a main thoroughfare, like main street.
And I was just sitting there and just sort of, like, amazed to see all these soldiers, you know, and how you put-- with [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] swastikas on their arms.
And I wasn't scared.
I was just looking at them and just-- thousands, I remember seeing, watching them going by.
And this unit, when they went right by me-- and one of the German soldier, he looked at me like where I was sitting and looking at them, and he gave me a big smile, which, I don't know.
I'm sure he didn't know I was a Jewish kid.
And, of course, I didn't know what was that all about.
But I still remember the smile he gave me.
And then all of a sudden, I guess my mother was looking for me in the house.
And she opened the door, and she saw me sitting on the porch, and she grabbed me into the house.
And of course, she told me not to go outside anymore, from here on, by myself, that I always have to stay in the house and be near them, near my parents or my sisters or brothers.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
And that's when troubles began with the Gestapos.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Like, do you want me to go in to-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Then we heard that Gestapos were coming into different villages and-- taking away Jewish people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then one day a neighbor came in.
It was in the morning.
And she told us-- she told my parents, the next village, the Gestapos are there.
They're taking away [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Jewish people.
They're rounding them up and taking them away to the city or the ghettos.
They didn't know where.
So my parents took us-- all the children.
And they said-- they said, we have to run away now.
And we ran through the back door through the fields, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] far away from the house into the fields, in high grains to hide.
And we were staying there, lying down in the grain until it got dark.
And then when darkness came-- evening-- my father said, OK, he's going to go to the house and check and see if the Gestapos were there.
And he went.
And he came back.
And he said, yeah, I spoke to the neighbor, and sure enough they did go through our house.
And nobody was in the house.
So he said, we can-- and they left our village, so we can go back.
So we went back to the house, and of course, they were all frightened.
I-- being a child, again, I was running, and I did what my parents asked me to do.
And this was happening several times like that, you know.
The neighbors would come in and say Gestapos are-- this neighbor.
They're looking for Jewish people.
And also Ukrainian [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] police.

They were collaborating with the Germans, and I guess they had an agreement that the Ukrainians will help the Germans to find all the Jewish people, because they knew exactly where the Jewish people were living-- which homes, which cities, which towns, which villages.
And they're going to help the Germans to to catch all the-- get all the Jewish people and kill them as well.
And so now we have to be careful of the Ukrainians, as well.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So this hiding, running away from the house, running into the fields, you know, happened a few times.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then, one morning, we were all home and we heard storming in into our house.
The doors were open.
And Gestapos came right-- walked right in.
There were maybe four or five of them.
And they saw my-- all of us, and they said, OK, all you men, you got to come forward.
And they grabbed my father and my two other brothers, and my youngest brother.
He was maybe 14 years old.
And he ran up to my mother.
And he took her around.
And he said, oh, Mom, I'm so scared.
Where are they taking me?
And my mother [SOB] took him around, and she says-- and she said to him, God will be with you, my son.
And then the Gestapo screamed out.
He says, OK, that's enough, let's go.
And-- and he went.
And they took all my brothers and my father outside.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SOBBING] And I went to the window, to the bedroom window, to look out where they take them.
And they-- they took them to a-- a-- wagon, a big wagon.
And with the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] rifles behind their necks.
And they told each one to go up to this big truck, or [NON-ENGLISH], whatever it was.
And I saw them going all up, with the Gestapos behind them in the doorway.
[SOBBING] And darkness took over the house.
It was a Sunday morning, but after that it was-- it was just unreal.
And my mother, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] God bless her, she took us around.
And I didn't hear her scream or yell.
She just was holding us, and praying, and praying.
[CRYING] And it was very bad, very tragic.
